programs.name: KanCare Medicaid
programs.tagline: Comprehensive healthcare and long-term care coverage for Kansas seniors who qualify for Medicaid.

localEntities.name: MCOs: Healthy Blue Kansas, Sunflower Health Plan, etc.

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ with limited income and assets, KanCare Medicaid covers all medical care plus long-term care services. This includes doctor visits, hospital stays, prescriptions, dental, vision, and either nursing home care or home-based services to help with daily activities like bathing and medication management.

The program has strict financial limits: single applicants can have only $2,000 in countable assets and must meet low-income thresholds. For nursing home care, nearly all of your parent's income goes toward care costs, but they can keep up to $2,901/month in income (2026). Because Kansas didn't expand Medicaid, this program primarily serves seniors 65+, people with disabilities, and families with children — not low-income adults under 65.

exemptAssets: Burial plots and funds up to $1,500
Life insurance with face value $1,500 or less

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: For long-term care services, your parent must need daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, or managing medications (called Nursing Facility Level of Care or NFLOC)
otherRequirements: Must use other health insurance first before KanCare

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at kancare.ks.gov or call 1-800-792-4884; processing takes 45-90 days.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect 60 days of bank statements, income proof, citizenship documents, and medical records

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply online at kancare.ks.gov, call 1-800-792-4884, or visit your local KDHE office

steps.title: Complete assessment if applying for long-term care
steps.description: A clinical assessor will evaluate your parent's need for daily care assistance

steps.title: Wait for decision
steps.description: Processing typically takes 45-90 days; you'll receive written notice of approval or denial

applicationGuide.processingTime: 45-90 days typical; varies by program complexity
applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible for Home and Community-Based Services waivers due to limited slots; nursing home coverage generally available if eligible
applicationGuide.tip: Apply as soon as your parent might need long-term care — even if they're not ready yet, getting on HCBS waiver waitlists early is crucial.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Life insurance with face value over $1,500
excluded: Burial plots and burial funds up to $1,500
Life insurance with face value $1,500 or less

tiers.name: Basic Aged, Blind, and Disabled (ABD)
tiers.coverage: Doctor visits, hospital, prescriptions, dental, vision

tiers.name: Nursing Home Medicaid
tiers.incomeLimit: Up to $2,901/month (nearly all goes toward care costs)
tiers.coverage: All medical care plus nursing facility room, board, and care

tiers.name: HCBS Waivers
tiers.coverage: Personal care, adult day care, home modifications, respite care

categories.name: Medical
items: Doctor's notes about daily care needs

contentSections.text: HCBS waiver slots are limited and have waitlists. Apply early even if your parent doesn't need services immediately — nursing home coverage is generally available, but home-based services may require waiting.
Kansas has a 5-year look-back period for asset transfers. If your parent gave away money or property in the past 5 years, it could create penalties that delay Medicaid coverage.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify for KanCare Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the home equity is under $713,000 and your parent intends to return home, or if a spouse or disabled child lives there. For nursing home residents, the home is protected if there's intent to return or certain family members live there.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: Kansas has a 'Medically Needy' program where your parent can 'spend down' excess income by paying medical bills. Once they've paid enough medical expenses, they can qualify for coverage.

faqs.question: My parent gave money to grandchildren last year. Will this affect eligibility?
faqs.answer: Possibly. Kansas looks back 5 years at all asset transfers. Gifts or below-market sales can create penalty periods that delay Medicaid coverage. You should disclose all transfers when applying.

faqs.question: What's the difference between nursing home coverage and home-based services?
faqs.answer: Both require the same level of care need (Nursing Facility Level of Care), but nursing home coverage is generally available immediately if eligible. Home-based services (HCBS waivers) have limited slots and waitlists, but allow your parent to stay home.

faqs.question: My parent has Medicare. Do they still need KanCare Medicaid?
faqs.answer: If your parent qualifies for both, KanCare works with Medicare to cover what Medicare doesn't — like long-term care, some prescriptions, and services Medicare limits. Your parent must use Medicare first, then KanCare covers additional costs.

faqs.question: How does this work if my parents are married?
faqs.answer: Special spousal impoverishment rules protect the community spouse (the one not needing care). The healthy spouse can keep more assets (around $154,140 in 2026) and income, so they're not left destitute while the other spouse gets Medicaid.

programs.name: KanCare Frail Elderly Waiver (FE Waiver)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs daily help but wants to avoid a nursing home, this Kansas program pays for home care services.

localEntities.name: Managed by Kansas Department for Aging and Disability Services (KDADS); providers enroll via fiscal agent (KMAP); delivered through Managed Care Organizations (MCOs) like Sunflower Health Plan

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay home, the KanCare Frail Elderly Waiver pays for home and community-based services as an alternative to nursing home placement. The program covers services like personal care, home modifications, and care coordination — but not room and board.

Your parent must first qualify for KanCare (Kansas Medicaid) and pass a functional assessment proving they need nursing facility-level care. Because slots are limited, there's often a waitlist. Your parent can keep $2,982 per month of their income (2026 amount), with the program covering authorized services through a personalized Plan of Care.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need nursing facility-level care based on Activities of Daily Living (bathing, dressing, toileting, transferring, mobility, eating) and cognitive/behavioral issues assessed through Functional Assessment Instrument
otherRequirements: Must qualify for KanCare Medicaid first
60-month look-back rule applies to asset transfers

applicationGuide.summary: Apply for KanCare Medicaid first, then contact KDADS for the waiver functional assessment — processing time varies due to waitlists.

steps.title: Apply for KanCare Medicaid
steps.description: Your parent must be eligible for Kansas Medicaid before applying for the waiver. Complete the KanCare application first.

steps.title: Contact KDADS for waiver screening
steps.description: Contact Kansas Department for Aging and Disability Services or your local waiver program manager to file a preliminary functional eligibility application.

steps.title: Complete functional assessment
steps.description: KDADS will conduct a Functional Assessment Instrument (FAI) to determine if your parent needs nursing facility-level care.

steps.title: Join waitlist if needed
steps.description: If no slots are available, your parent will be placed on the proposed recipient list until an opening occurs.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Waitlist maintained when no openings exist due to limited slots
applicationGuide.tip: Start the KanCare Medicaid application immediately — you cannot get the waiver without qualifying for Medicaid first.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Assets transferred for less than fair market value in past 60 months
excluded: Primary home (if equity is $752,000 or less and parent lives there or intends to return)

categories.name: Functional Assessment
items: Medical records showing need for daily assistance
Documentation of Activities of Daily Living limitations
Cognitive or behavioral assessment records if applicable

contentSections.text: Limited slots mean waitlists are common. Even if your parent qualifies, they may not receive services immediately.
The 60-month look-back rule penalizes asset transfers below fair market value. Don't transfer assets without consulting an elder law attorney first.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for the waiver?
faqs.answer: Yes, as long as the home equity is $752,000 or less (2026 limit) and she lives there or intends to return. The house won't count toward asset limits.

faqs.question: What if Dad has too much income for regular Medicaid?
faqs.answer: The waiver allows your parent to keep $2,982 per month (2026 amount). Income above this goes toward the cost of care, but they may still qualify through other KanCare categories or spend-down provisions.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist for services?
faqs.answer: Waitlist times vary by region and aren't specified in official sources. Contact your local waiver program manager for current wait times in your area.

faqs.question: What happens if Mom gave away money to family in the past 5 years?
faqs.answer: The 60-month look-back rule creates penalty periods for asset transfers below fair market value. This could delay eligibility, so gather documentation of any transfers or gifts.

faqs.question: Does the waiver pay for Mom's apartment rent?
faqs.answer: No, the waiver doesn't cover room and board or living expenses like rent, mortgage, or groceries. It only pays for authorized home and community-based services.

faqs.question: What if Dad doesn't qualify for the functional assessment?
faqs.answer: If the Functional Assessment Instrument shows your parent doesn't need nursing facility-level care, they won't qualify for the waiver. However, they may be eligible for other KanCare services or community programs.

programs.name: KanCare PACE
programs.tagline: Complete medical care coordination for parents 55+ who need nursing-home-level help but want to stay at home.

localEntities.name: Unnamed provider: Marion, McPherson, Ottawa, Reno, Rice, Saline counties
Midland Care PACE: Wyandotte, Leavenworth, Shawnee, Douglas, Jackson, Jefferson, Osage, Pottawatomie counties

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and needs significant daily help but wants to avoid a nursing home, PACE provides all their medical care under one coordinated team. Your parent gets primary care, specialists, physical therapy, transportation to appointments, meals, social activities, and home support services — all managed by a single provider instead of juggling multiple doctors and services.

PACE is only available in specific Kansas counties, and your parent must qualify for nursing-home-level care (meaning they need daily help with things like bathing, dressing, or medication management). If your parent has Medicare and Medicaid, or income under about $3,000/month, there's typically no cost. Private pay options exist for those with higher incomes.

Because PACE covers everything from routine checkups to emergency care, your parent won't need to coordinate between separate providers — one team manages their complete care plan and adjusts services as their needs change.

summary: Must be able to live safely at home
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified by Kansas as needing nursing facility level of care (a clinical assessment of whether your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other activities of daily living)
otherRequirements: Must live in a PACE service area
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage or hospice
Must be able to live safely in the community at enrollment

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local PACE provider directly — they'll handle the application and nursing facility assessment.

steps.title: Find your PACE provider
steps.description: Check if your parent lives in a covered county and get the contact for that area's PACE organization.

steps.title: Call the provider
steps.description: They'll schedule an assessment to determine if your parent qualifies for nursing facility level of care.

steps.title: Complete KDADS assessment
steps.description: Kansas Department for Aging and Disability Services must certify your parent needs nursing-home-level care.

steps.title: Enrollment decision
steps.description: If approved, the PACE team creates your parent's individualized care plan and begins services.

applicationGuide.tip: Start with your PACE provider first — they'll guide you through the state assessment process and help gather required documents.

offices.name: Midland Care PACE
offices.address: Wyandotte, Leavenworth, Shawnee, Douglas, Jackson, Jefferson, Osage, Pottawatomie counties

offices.name: Kansas Department for Aging and Disability Services
offices.address: For nursing facility level of care assessment

categories.name: Medical
items: Recent medical records or doctor assessments

contentSections.text: PACE is only available in specific counties. Most of Kansas is not covered — check your county before starting the application.
Your parent cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage (Part C) to qualify for PACE. They'll need to switch to Original Medicare first.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is too high for Medicaid but they can't afford private insurance?
faqs.answer: PACE has private pay options for people who don't qualify for Medicaid. About 10% of PACE participants pay privately. Contact your local PACE provider for current premium rates.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their current doctors?
faqs.answer: No. PACE provides all medical care through their own team of doctors, nurses, and specialists. Your parent will get new primary care providers, but PACE coordinates all their care in one place.

faqs.question: What if my parent needs to go to the nursing home later?
faqs.answer: PACE covers nursing facility care when needed. Because they already manage your parent's care, the transition is coordinated by the same team rather than starting over with new providers.

faqs.question: How much help does my parent need to qualify for 'nursing facility level of care'?
faqs.answer: Generally, your parent needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility. Kansas will do a clinical assessment to determine if they meet this level of need.

faqs.question: What happens if we move to a county without PACE?
faqs.answer: Your parent would lose PACE coverage if they move outside the service area. They'd need to find new Medicare and Medicaid providers in their new location.

faqs.question: Does PACE provide transportation to appointments?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE includes medical transportation as part of their services, so your parent gets rides to appointments, the day center, and other PACE activities.
